findings.claim: ADR 0040, 0041, 0042, and 0043 form a four-record recurring-revision chain around one unresolved primitive: no build-time (or, later, placement-time) measure can discriminate an intentional connector-bridge overlap from an actual glyph weld defect. Each record explicitly traces back to this same discrimination problem, and each one parks, banks, or defers it rather than closing it -- ADR 0040 parks the milestone on this exact problem, ADR 0041 names it 'the same root as ADR 0040', ADR 0042 banks the dependent thin-hand fix at ~B pending a placement rework, and ADR 0043 (the most recent) still ends parked on 'one remaining problem' that is explicitly identified as ADR 0040's discrimination problem recurring in a new form. Four Accepted records, none resolving the shared question.

citations.quote: no build-time measure can tell that intentional overlap from a body weld, and the gap the eye actually reads does not exist until the glyphs are ASSEMBLED
Both rejected attempts are a build-time proxy diverging from the render — the same root as ADR 0040: no build-time measure separates the connector bridge from a weld.
Phase 3 is a major, risky rework, so BANK thin-hand join evenness at ~B and defer it to a focused milestone.
It is PARKED at one remaining problem: a placement-aware protection layer that tells a deliberate thin bridge from a body weld.

findings.consequence: A maintainer scanning the log by title would see four seemingly distinct milestones (kern refinement, dense-body kern, Phase 1 height, Phase 3 placement) and could easily miss that they are four attempts at the same unresolved bridge-vs-weld discrimination problem, none of which has shipped a fix -- the field defect (thin-hand join evenness) remains banked at ~B across all four records with no closing decision.
